data.meaning_mnemonic: A turtle that stands up isn't a turtle anymore. It's evolved into a monster. What kind of monster? A <radical>dragon</radical>! Rawwwr.
This radical is the same as the kanji. It means <radical>wind</radical>.
The <radical>measurement</radical> radical and the <kanji>measurement</kanji> kanji are exactly the same.
data.meaning_hint: If you make sure to know the radicals, you'll also make sure to know the kanji too, automagically.
data.reading_mnemonic: "Calculate your enemy's position with accurate <kanji>measurement</kanji>," is more or less what <reading>Sun</reading> (すん) Tzu said, I think. The guy wrote The Art of War, you know. When Sun says measurements are important, you don't question him.
data.reading_hint: Imagine Mr. Sun measuring distances and rations and whatnot just before going to war — maps, compasses, all kinds of measurement devices piled on his war table. Also, don't forget that in Chinese Sun is pronounced more like "soon."

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>dragon</radical> radical is the same as this kanji, which is <kanji>dragon</kanji>.
data.meaning_hint: Not to <kanji>dragon</kanji> with it, but make sure you know your radicals so you know your kanji!
data.reading_mnemonic: Imagine a <kanji>dragon</kanji>. You are talking to it, which is already pretty strange, but as you're talking, he turns into the Street Fighter character <reading>Ryu</reading> (りゅう).
data.reading_hint: Then imagine after his transformation he attacks you using his Street Fighter moves. "Hadouken!"

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>thread</radical> is all that's keeping <radical>Wolverine</radical> and a <radical>pig</radical> from falling off the <kanji>edge</kanji> of a cliff. Wolverine and this pig had no <kanji>relation</kanji> prior to this, but after such a harrowing experience they're sure to form a deep bond.
data.meaning_hint: Picture Wolverine and a pig hanging precariously over the edge of this cliff. What a crazy way to form a relation! It'll all be for naught if the thread snaps, though…
data.reading_mnemonic: Just as it seems like they'll be able to climb back from the <kanji>edge</kanji>, the thread snaps, and Wolverine and his new pig friend plunge to a tragic <reading>en</reading>d (えん). What a shame — they had just begun to form such a friendly <kanji>relation</kanji>, too…
data.reading_hint: Moral of the story: stay away from edges, because they might spell the end for you and all your earthly relations.

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>feather</radical> is on a <radical>rice paddy</radical>, and they are attached <radical>together</radical>. You put a feather on a rice paddy to create a giant airplane. Flawed, sure. But, this rice paddy + feather is going to be one <kanji>wing</kanji> of your amazing aircraft.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine a <kanji>wing</kanji> that is made of a rice paddy and a feather put together. Doesn't make sense, looks ugly, and probably will not fly. Imagine it anyways. It is your wing.
data.reading_mnemonic: To make the feather and rice paddy stick together you need some kind of sticking agent. This time we find the original bird that the feather came from and grab its eggs. We break the eggs open and use the <reading>yolk</reading> (よく). It's pretty sticky, but still probably not aircraft safe.
data.reading_hint: Imagine using the yolks to stick everything together. It's all over your hands, and things are pretty nasty.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>good luck</radical> radical and the <kanji>good luck</kanji> kanji are the same!
data.meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
data.reading_mnemonic: For <kanji>good luck</kanji>, you keep special <reading>kits</reading> (きつ) in your <reading>kitch</reading>en (きち). These kitchen kits contain good luck charms.
data.reading_hint: Your good luck kitchen kits have charms that say things like, "good food," "good company," and "good luck not cutting off your fingers with kitchen knives."

data.meaning_mnemonic: The radical <radical>blade</radical> and the kanji <kanji>blade</kanji> are exactly the same.
data.meaning_hint: Please, just know your radicals. Please!
data.reading_mnemonic: When you stab your <kanji>blade</kanji> into an enemy you shout "<reading>Hah</reading> (は)!" You've gotta get some oomph behind that thrust, after all.
data.reading_hint: Hah! Slash! Hah! Thrust! Hah! Parry! There you go — now you're really using that blade!

data.meaning_mnemonic: If someone puts a <radical>blade</radical> to your <radical>heart</radical>, you're going to have a rough time. They leave it there, for a long time. They aren't going to cut you, but you can't move. You have to <kanji>endure</kanji> the pain and <kanji>conceal</kanji> your discomfort with the situation.
data.meaning_hint: To endure something is to conceal how it makes you feel, after all. Now imagine there's a blade pressed close to your heart. Try to endure the pain and conceal your emotions, or your heart might be cut.
data.reading_mnemonic: Learning to <kanji>endure</kanji> and <kanji>conceal</kanji> is all part of your <reading>nin</reading>ja (にん) training. You must learn to stay totally still, without breathing — you have to be able to endure this if you want to conceal yourself.
data.reading_hint: No one knows better than a ninja how enduring and concealing are closely related. Go ahead, hold your breath and try walking soundlessly around your house, concealing your footsteps. Not easy to endure, is it?

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>tree</radical> has an <radical>omen</radical> growing from it, one single <kanji>peach</kanji> that is the size of a house. Inside is a child who will save the village from demons.
data.meaning_hint: Of course, this tree omen of a <kanji>peach</kanji> refers to momotaro, the little boy who was born from a peach (you should look it up if you don't know this children's story).
data.reading_mnemonic: If <reading>momo</reading>taro (もも) wasn't enough, think about the <kanji>peach</kanji> growing larger and larger. You want it to grow bigger, so you yell at it: "<reading>MO</reading>RE! <reading>MO</reading>RE!" (もも).
data.reading_hint: It grows bigger everytime you say "MORE MORE" twice. If you say it just once, it stays the same.

data.meaning_mnemonic: No one can <radical>say</radical> what a <radical>scooter</radical> weighs in grains of <radical>rice</radical> — it's a <kanji>mystery</kanji>!
data.meaning_hint: Imagine lifting a scooter, hefting it, trying to guess its weight in grains of rice. At least 100 grains, I'd say. But no one can say, really. It's a total mystery.
data.reading_mnemonic: To get to the bottom of this mystery, you use a nozzle (なぞ). You pass rice through the nozzle one grain at a time, so you can figure out precisely how many grains of rice are equal to the weight of a scooter.
data.reading_hint: Imagine watching this little nozzle squeeze out the rice, grain by grain, onto a scale. And imagine the boredom you'd feel watching that… But yes, you really can resolve any rice-based measurement mysteries thanks to this nozzle.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of the <radical>temple</radical> isn't a monk like you'd expect. It's a <kanji>samurai</kanji>, who protects the monks in return for prayers that keep him safe.
data.meaning_hint: You walk up to a temple and you're surprised to find that there's a samurai guarding it. Talk to him for a while so he can tell you why he has been doing this job. He's quite a nice guy, actually.
data.reading_mnemonic: The reading is <reading>samurai</reading>, which should be easy enough to remember.
data.reading_hint: If you know the meaning you know the reading as well! That doesn't happen too often with kanji, so consider yourself lucky!

data.meaning_mnemonic: From my <radical>mouth</radical> I have told you <radical>seven</radical> times not to do that. Now I have to <kanji>scold</kanji> you.
data.meaning_hint: SEVEN TIMES! You'd think you'd learn.
data.reading_mnemonic: What were you doing wrong that caused me to <kanji>scold</kanji> you? You were feeding the <reading>鹿</reading> (しか). If you feed them they'll never be able to feed themselves!
data.reading_hint: Imagine yourself feeding the deer, then getting scolded (or you doing the scolding to someone else feeding the deer, whichever works best).

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>woman</radical> loves her <radical>catapult</radical> for some good ol' fashioned <kanji>recreation</kanji>.
data.meaning_hint: You ask the woman what she does for fun... for recreation. She says, she goes out and shoots her catapult.
data.reading_mnemonic: Her favorite thing to shoot at with her catapult for <kanji>recreation</kanji> is <reading>Go</reading>dzilla (ご).
data.reading_hint: Get out of here, Godzilla! I'm gonna shoot you.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>ladle</radical> radical and the <kanji>ladle</kanji> kanji are the same!
data.meaning_hint: Know your radicals, yo!
data.reading_mnemonic: You use a <kanji>ladle</kanji> to distribute soup. But as you're ladling you find something odd floating in the soup. It's... a <reading>toe</reading> (と)! You scream, dropping the ladle and the toe on the ground.
data.reading_hint: Your mom comes rushing in and smacks you on the head. "Fool!" she says, "This is toe soup, what did you think you'd ladle up?!" Oops.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>slide</radical> to get off this <radical>jet</radical> is a beautiful, brilliant <kanji>vermilion</kanji> color.
data.meaning_hint: Vermilion is like an orange red, which is perfect for visibility purposes.
data.reading_mnemonic: You jump onto the <kanji>vermilion</kanji> slide. It's actually more of a steep <reading>chu</reading>te (しゅ). And... oh... urp... it's making you sick.
data.reading_hint: The vermilion chute spits you out and you spit out your lunch. And it looks vermilion. Uh oh. Hospital. Please.

data.meaning_mnemonic: An <radical>axe</radical> is stuck into the <radical>ground</radical> on a <kanji>hill</kanji>.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine climbing up the hill. The sun is coming up behind the axe,  silhouetting the whole thing very nicely.
data.reading_mnemonic: You reach the top of the <kanji>hill</kanji> and realize the entire hill is covered in <reading>cu</reading>cumbers (きゅう). The axe has a little note on it that says "Cucumber Hill Axe - Please Use."
data.reading_hint: You take the axe out of the hill and cut off some cucumber to eat. Mm mm!

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>grain</radical> you cut off a <radical>tree</radical> with a <radical>knife</radical> is a <kanji>pear</kanji>. You've found some particularly big, sturdy ones, which is why you need the help of your knife. These pears are sure to be super juicy and delicious, but you need some extra tools to get them off the tree.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine looking up at a tree full of thick-stemmed, jumbo-size pears. You pull out your knife and get ready to hack them all down, one by one.
data.reading_mnemonic: As you cut the <kanji>pear</kanji>s down, you can't help but <reading>gnash</reading> (なし) your teeth. You want to eat these pears right now. You gnash and grind and wait though, because you want to have them all down before you start the feast.
data.reading_hint: Imagine a beautiful, ripe pear in your mind. Now gnash your teeth. Mmm. You want to eat a pear right now, don't you?

data.meaning_mnemonic: You have <radical>mouth</radical> <radical>vines</radical>, so you <kanji>shout</kanji>! It's hard for you to talk normally, and even harder for people to understand you unless you shout.
data.meaning_hint: You were born with mouth vines (a rare condition where your mouth is always full of vines), so you've been shouting all your life. And yet people still can't hear you. It's very frustrating.
data.reading_mnemonic: You need to <kanji>shout</kanji> so that people can understand you through the mouth vines. You shout, "<reading>きょう</reading>と!" because you hear there is a temple there for people with your condition.
data.reading_hint: Imagine the word きょうと not coming out clearly because of your mouth vines. People can only hear the きょう part, so they don't know where you want to go. SHOUT LOUDER! きょうと！

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>gold</radical> <radical>pool</radical> is where you're going to go <kanji>fishing</kanji>. Because, if the pool is gold, hopefully the fish are too.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine yourself fishing in a gold pool, catching golden fish. What a great <kanji>fishing</kanji> spot this is.
data.reading_mnemonic: You caught a couple of gold fish. <reading>Two</reading> (つ) gold fish, actually!
data.reading_hint: Count your golden fish. They're so nice! They're also worth their weight in gold, because, well, they are gold.

data.meaning_mnemonic: Your <radical>long</radical> <radical>hair</radical> is your <radical>friend</radical>. It's your <kanji>hair</kanji> and you're so proud of it.
data.meaning_hint: Just feel your hair, it's such a good friend, and you wish it was longer.
data.reading_mnemonic: Who gave you this hair? Probably the great <reading>神</reading> (かみ) of hair. You deserve this great hair.
data.reading_hint: Pretend to pray to the great hair 神. What is his reply? Does he give you more hair?

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>mountain</radical> <radical>wind</radical> coming down from the mountains is very fierce. It is so fierce it's a lot like a <kanji>storm</kanji>. Well, it is a storm, really.
data.meaning_hint: If you think about it makes sense. Those mountain winds that come down from the mountain are cold and windy and nasty. When they mix with warmer air down below, it creates a nasty <kanji>storm</kanji>.
data.reading_mnemonic: A <kanji>storm</kanji> is nothing to scoff at. If you get hit by the storm and get all wet and nasty and electrocuted, you're bound to get <reading>a rash</reading> (あらし).
data.reading_hint: The rash comes from being wet and not drying off when you get home. Your skin gets irritated by this moisture.

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>bamboo</radical> thing you put on your head to <radical>stand</radical> taller would probably be a hat. A bamboo hat is probably one of those hats you think of when you think of rice farmers, a <kanji>conical hat</kanji>.
data.meaning_hint: You know the ones I'm talking about? They're cone-shaped, sort of, and they go on your head, keeping the sun off your face.
data.reading_mnemonic: A <kanji>conical hat</kanji> is kind of like an umbrella on your head. What's the reading for "umbrella" in Japanese? Why, it's <reading>kasa</reading> (かさ)!
data.reading_hint: Think about a bamboo umbrella on your head. They're pretty similar in shape, after all.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>woman</radical> <radical>servant</radical> is the lower ranking <kanji>princess</kanji>. The queen is no servant. Hecks no. Just the princess, because she's a servant to what her parents tell her to do.
data.meaning_hint: Just imagine a princess saying "UGH, I'm just like a servant, I have to do all the crap you tell me." Then, all the servants roll their eyes, because, really, she's a princess, lucky duck.
data.reading_mnemonic: The <kanji>princess</kanji> goes on to do whatever she wants. She tells the real servants, "Don't tell my daddy, the king, I did this. <reading>He may</reading> (ひめ) not like it."
data.reading_hint: The servants all know they should tell the king, because the princess is a servant to the king, just as they are, plus they don't want to lose their heads.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The part of <radical>rice</radical> that <radical>stand</radical>s up tall in the air (at the end of the stalks) is the <kanji>grains</kanji>.
data.meaning_hint: If you think about this one it'll make good sense. Just imagine rice stalks standing up into the air.
data.reading_mnemonic: You have a box of <kanji>grains</kanji>. <reading>Ryuu</reading> (りゅう) from Street Fighter is doing training by punching the grains to strengthen his fingers.
data.reading_hint: He has his hands flat, and stabs his hands into the grains. Yah! Yah! Yah!

data.meaning_mnemonic: You put a <radical>stone</radical> into a <radical>wrap</radical> of some kind. This wrap happens to be a <kanji>cannon</kanji>, and you're shooting off the stone, just like I used to do to boats with the cannon we had growing up for some reason.
data.meaning_hint: This makes a lot of sense when you think about it, right? The muzzle of the cannon wraps around the rock, then you shoot the rock. Boom!
data.reading_mnemonic: You aim the <kanji>cannon</kanji> at your own <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). You don't want to have a home anymore, you want to become a pirate, live on a boat, and (of course) shoot cannons all day long.
data.reading_hint: Watch as the cannon goes off, the rock hurtles into your home. And just like that, there is a huge, rock-shaped hole in it. You'll have to shoot a few more rocks at your home with this cannon before it's completely gone.

data.meaning_mnemonic: <radical>Rain</radical> on a <radical>rice paddy</radical> means only one thing... <kanji>thunder</kanji>! Get off the rice paddy! There's nothing but you to attract the thunder!
data.meaning_hint: Imagine hearing the thunder closing in on you as you frantically try to escape the rice paddy. RUN!!!
data.reading_mnemonic: You can’t run fast enough to escape the <kanji>thunder</kanji>, so you decide to hide under a pile of… <reading>rye</reading> (らい)?!
data.reading_hint: This rye will save your life. It’s just you and this pile of rye now. Take a deeeeep breath in, and really smell the thunder and rye in the air. You’re fine. You’re cozy. This rye will be your savior.

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <radical>flowers</radical> <radical>fang</radical> is the bud that comes out of the ground. It's like a little fang, coming from the earth, and it will become a flower someday. This is a little <kanji>sprout</kanji>.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine a bunch of these. They look like big fangs poking out of the ground (that is, until they bloom and get into the air).
data.reading_mnemonic: When the <kanji>sprout</kanji> gets out of the ground and opens up, instead of a flower there is a human-looking <reading>目</reading> (め), looking right at you.
data.reading_hint: You look at all the sprouts. They have all turned into 目s, looking straight at you. You're terrified.

data.meaning_mnemonic: When <radical>dirt</radical> and <radical>flowers</radical> <radical>suit</radical> one another, the flowers grow so tall that they make a <kanji>tower</kanji>! A flower tower made of flower power!
data.meaning_hint: Imagine walking in the woods and coming across a huge tower made of flowers. It goes so high you can hardly see the top.
data.reading_mnemonic: The only place flowers grow into <kanji>tower</kanji>s like this is in <reading>とう</reading>きょう. とうきょう dirt is the best. とうきょう flowers are the best. Thus, you end up with a lot of とうきょう towers.
data.reading_hint: How do you think the とうきょう tower came to be, anyway? It was once a big flower tower. They just replaced it with metal later. BOOM! History bomb, you're welcome.

data.meaning_mnemonic: The <radical>animal</radical> that hangs out in <radical>melon</radical> patches is the elusive <kanji>fox</kanji>! They like to steal melons then come back into town disguised as human melon sellers. Foxes are tricky like that.
data.meaning_hint: Imagine seeing a red fox scampering through a melon patch trying to take melons with it.
data.reading_mnemonic: If you want to stop <kanji>fox</kanji>es from stealing your melons you can get one of those <reading>kits</reading> that <reading>neigh</reading> (きつね) when one is around. The kits neigh when they sense a fox, but because it sounds like a horse the fox won't run. But you'll be able to get out there and take your melons back!
data.reading_hint: Imagine buying kits that neigh and putting them around your melon patch to warn you when foxes are around.

data.meaning_mnemonic:
When you <kanji>help</kanji> with your <kanji>power</kanji>, you are providing <vocabulary>assistance</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>support</vocabulary>.

Note that this is a formal word that’s mostly used in professional situations. It implies that someone has done something substantial for you.
data.reading_mnemonic:
This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. Since 力 has two on'yomi readings, here's a mnemonic to help you remember which one to use:

You really need some <vocabulary>assistance</vocabulary> with this <reading>lock</reading> (りょく). You are certain that world peace and eternal happiness lie behind this one lock. But no one is willing to <vocabulary>support</vocabulary> you in opening it. What a cruel world.

context_sentences.en: That's thanks to help of my father.
context_sentences.ja: 父の助力のおかげです。

context_sentences.en: This was only possible with the help of others.
context_sentences.ja: 他人の助力が有ったから出来たことです。

context_sentences.en: With the help of a friend, it seems like I can get out of college after four years.
context_sentences.ja: 友人の助力で、四年間で大学を出ることが出来そうです。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
You are <kanji>alone</kanji> in what you are able to <kanji>create</kanji>. Nobody else can create like you. So what you create is your own <vocabulary>original creation</vocabulary>.

独創 refers to the act of <vocabulary>creating something original</vocabulary>, or the resulting creation itself. This word is quite formal, and generally used as 独創的 (original) or 独創性 (originality).
data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word, which usually means on'yomi readings from the kanji. If you know the readings of your kanji you'll know how to read this as well.

context_sentences.en: It’s so disappointing that he is lacking in originality.
context_sentences.ja: 彼が独創性に欠けていることは非常に残念です。

context_sentences.en: The more creative your idea is, the more people come out against it.
context_sentences.ja: アイディアが独創的であれば独創的であるほど、反対する人も多いよね。

context_sentences.en: WaniKani is President Koichi's original creation.
context_sentences.ja: ワニカニはコウイチ社長の独創です。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji itself means <kanji>sink</kanji> so the verb vocab version is <vocabulary>to sink something</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to submerge something</vocabulary>.

This is a transitive verb, meaning it takes a direct object. So instead of describing something sinking on its own, you use 沈める when you sink an enemy ship or submerge yourself in a bathtub. 

You know this because you can't sink anything until you get that <reading>mail</reading> (める) saying, "Yes, proceed with sinking!" You've got to have that mail if you want to sink something, or else everyone will blame you for the missing ship — or for you disappearing under the water.
data.reading_mnemonic: When you sink something, like a battleship, you go on and say: "<reading>she's</reading> (しず) sunk, I sunk her." That's like your tagline, or something. You say it for some reason because you think it's cool.

context_sentences.en: He is watching the broadcast of the Academy Awards ceremony and sinking into the sofa.
context_sentences.ja: 彼はソファに身を沈めながら、アカデミー賞の授賞式の生中継を観ています。

data.meaning_mnemonic: You know how 携帯 refers to a mobile phone? This is a <kanji>strap</kanji> (ストラップ) for a mobile phone, making this a <vocabulary>cell phone strap</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>mobile phone strap</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. You should be able to read this on your own. Will help to know the reading of 携帯.

context_sentences.en: The cell phone strap is so gross just thinking about it makes me puke.
context_sentences.ja: あの携帯ストラップが気色悪すぎて、考えるだけでも吐きそうだわ。

data.meaning_mnemonic: 克服 is "conquest" so the verb version of that is <vocabulary>to conquer</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. You should be able to read this on your own.

context_sentences.en: I’ve been afraid of dogs since I was bitten by one when I was three, but I’ve overcome my hatred for them since meeting this little puppy.
context_sentences.ja: 三歳の頃犬に噛まれてから、ずっと犬に恐怖心を抱いていたんですが、この小さな仔犬に出会って犬嫌いを克服しました。

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <kanji>philosophy</kanji> <kanji>scholar</kanji> (学者) is someone who studies philosophy. They're known as a <vocabulary>philosopher</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. You should be able to read this on your own.

context_sentences.en: The philosopher had to hang on to his beret as he walked through the strong wind.
context_sentences.ja: その哲学者は強風を通って歩いたので、自分のベレー帽をしっかりと掴まなくてはならなかった。

data.meaning_mnemonic: Your <kanji>mourning</kanji> <kanji>clothes</kanji> is your <vocabulary>mourning dress</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>mourning clothes</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic: The reading comes from the on'yomi for 服 and the kun'yomi for 喪, which you learned with the word 喪. You should know how to read this word!

context_sentences.en: My sister is anorexic and when wearing her mourning dress, she looked even skinnier.
context_sentences.ja: 私の妹は拒食症なのですが、喪服を着ているとより一層痩せ細ってみえました。

data.meaning_mnemonic: A <kanji>challenge</kanji> (挑戦) <kanji>someone</kanji> is someone who is going to challenge you. They are <vocabulary>a challenger</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. You should be able to read this on your own.

context_sentences.en: The arm‐wrestling challenger crushed an apple in his right hand, showing off the strength of his right hand.
context_sentences.ja: 腕相撲の挑戦者は、握力を見せつけるために、右手で林檎を潰してみせました。

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Remember how 揚げる means "to deep fry"? This is the prefix or suffix version of that word, making it <vocabulary>deep-fried</vocabulary>...

Note that 揚げ can sometimes be used as a standalone word, in which case it usually refers to deep-fried tofu.
data.reading_mnemonic: The reading comes from 揚げる as well.

context_sentences.en: Fried donuts, fried bananas, fried dumplings… I just love deep-fried food.

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Remember how 握る is "to grasp"? This is the noun version and means <vocabulary>one's grip</vocabulary>. It's often used to talk about how strong or loose someone's grip is, like on a tool, a baseball, or even an opponent's collar in judo.

If you add a number to 握り, like in 一握り, it can mean <vocabulary>handful</vocabulary>. It can also refer to <vocabulary>nigiri sushi</vocabulary>, since making that kind of sushi involves taking a handful of rice and gripping it into shape.

Occasionally this can also refer to something you're meant to grip, like the handle on a fishing rod or a pachinko machine, but this use is less common. Whew, there's a lot to know about this word — but hopefully you've got a grip on 握り now.
data.reading_mnemonic: The reading comes from 握る, so get a grip on that!

data.meaning_mnemonic:
Remember how 焦げる means "to get burned"? 焦がす is the transitive version, so this time someone is actively doing the burning, making it <vocabulary>to burn</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>to scorch</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>to char</vocabulary>.

焦がす means to burn the surface of something, usually blackening it to some degree. Unlike 焦げる, it can be for both intentional burning (a nice crème brûlée) and accidental burning (leaving bread in the toaster too long, scorching your sleeve on the stove).

data.meaning_mnemonic:
If you want to <kanji>distract</kanji> someone, try saying something <vocabulary>confusing</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>misleading</vocabulary> to them. They'll be so busy trying confusedly to interpret your comment that they'll become totally unable to focus on whatever it is they were originally doing.

紛らわしい is an adjective for things which are confusing because they're <vocabulary>easy to mix up</vocabulary> or easy to be misled by. Think similar-looking kanji (末 and 未, anyone?), places with the same name (London, as in England or Canada?), and celebrities you just can't seem to tell apart. Sometimes, it can refer to intentionally deceptive things too, like a button on a website tricking you into signing up for something. Either way, if it's easy to confuse for something else, then you can probably say it's 紛らわしい.
data.reading_mnemonic:
You already learned 紛らす, and this uses the same reading, but here's a mnemonic in case you need a reminder:

You know who is particularly <vocabulary>confusing</vocabulary>? That <reading>Maggie</reading> (まぎ) girl. She's confusing because her name keeps switching between Margaret and Maggie mid-conversation. You think she prefers Maggie, but at this point, you're honestly not even sure anymore.

data.reading_mnemonic:
The reading is the same as the one you learned with the kanji. But it also has a secondary reading you haven't learned, so here’s a mnemonic for it:

<vocabulary>Blade</vocabulary> in hand, you charge at your foes hollering "<reading>Yah</reading>! <reading>Eee</reading>! <reading>Baa</reading>!" (やいば). As the blade cuts through them, you cry "Yah! Eee! Baa!" even louder. This battle cry always fuels your fury and doubles the force of your swinging blade. "Yah! Eee! Baa!"

While both readings relate to blades, they have slightly different uses. は is used for any cutting tool's blade or edge, whereas やいば is a little old-fashioned and specifically refers to the blades of swords or knives, often in literary contexts.

朱 refers to <vocabulary>vermilion</vocabulary>, a bright red color with a slight orange tint, and can also be translated as <vocabulary>scarlet red</vocabulary>. It's common in Japanese culture — you'll often see it on shrine gates and lacquerware, for example.
data.reading_mnemonic:
The reading can be either the on'yomi or kun'yomi reading, so you have a choice there. Since you learned the on'yomi しゅ with the kanji already, let's learn the あけ reading now.

あけ sounds like the beginning of 開ける (to open). So imagine you <reading>開け</reading>る (あけ) a person up, to see what color they are inside. Turns out it's <vocabulary>vermilion</vocabulary>! Uh… you shouldn't open people up like that.

朱 is mostly read しゅ, but it can also be read あけ in certain phrases, like 朱に染まる, which means "to be covered in blood" (literally, "to be dyed in vermilion").

data.meaning_mnemonic:
This word consists of kanji with hiragana attached. Because the hiragana ends with an う sound, you know this word is a verb. The kanji means <kanji>split</kanji>, so this verb has to do with something splitting. 

This is an intransitive verb, so this verb means <vocabulary>to be split</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>to be torn</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>to burst apart</vocabulary>.
data.reading_mnemonic:
Since this word has okurigana (hiragana attached to the kanji) you know that it's probably going to be the kun'yomi reading.

Lucky for you, the kanji readings for transitive and intransitive verbs are almost always the same. Once you learn one, you know them both! This reading is the same as 裂く.

data.meaning_mnemonic:
A <kanji>one</kanji> <kanji>ladle</kanji> <kanji>can</kanji> is a <vocabulary>eighteen-liter container</vocabulary>, which is also a <vocabulary>five-gallon container</vocabulary>. That must be one big ladle!

If you live in Japan you've probably seen these. They're rectangular shaped containers made of metal that are used mostly for industrial purposes, for storing liquids like paint, fuel and chemicals. The 斗 actually refers to an old Japanese measurement, which is roughly equivalent to eighteen liters.
data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. Just watch out for the 一, which gets shortened to いっ.

data.meaning_mnemonic:
<kanji>Go upstream</kanji> through the currents of time to <kanji>reach</kanji> something in the past — this is called <vocabulary>being retroactive</vocabulary>. 

遡及 is a formal, technical word used when something is applied retroactively. You'll mostly see it in legal or administrative contexts when discussing rules or effects that are being applied to past actions or situations.
data.reading_mnemonic: This is a jukugo word that uses the on'yomi readings of the kanji. You know these readings — no need to retroactively learn any new readings for old kanji here.
